dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for a traffic/transportation incident at Jack Rabbits parking lot.
Hi, I'm Derek, an EMS EMT. What made you call 911 today?
We understand there was a motorcycle crash in the area.
Yeah, I was in an accident. Got some bad scrapes and aches.
Got it, your main symptoms are pain from the bike crash. That's your chief complaint.
Let's check your alertness. What's your name and where are we?
I'm Mike and we are in the parking lot of Jack Rabbits.
Good. What time is it today?
September 9th, 2020.
Great. What happened that led you to call 911?
I was on my motorcycle and a car pulled out of nowhere. I crashed and now I'm in a lot of pain.
Can you keep your eyes wide open? Can you follow my finger with each eye?
Eyes are open and tracking your finger just fine.
What specific symptoms did you experience when you were in the accident?
My arms, hands, and legs are sore, and I'm in a lot of pain.
Can you show me your right foot and left hand, please?
Sure, my right foot is here and my left hand is here.
Your airway is clear, and you're breathing on your own.
Your breathing is 16, easy, and unlabored.
I'm placing this on your chest, is it okay if we listen to your breath?
Okay, but I don't have any trouble breathing.
Is your breathing equal on both sides?
Yes, I can move both sides of my chest the same.
Your pulse is 69, strong and regular.
You're not pale or diaphoretic. 26. Primary Assessment; check circulation: Blood Pressure; Partner: Let's get your blood pressure - 184 over 114.
SpO₂ is 97 percent.
Are you currently experiencing dizziness, nausea, or chest pain?

I don't have any of that just hurts from the fall. 30. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; EMT: Are you having any headache or abdominal pain? 31. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: No to all of it, just the pain from the crash.
Any vomiting? 33. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: No, not at all.
Do you have any medical conditions?
I have mild asthma.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how bad is the pain? 37. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.: severity; Patient: It's a solid 10.

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Can you explain what happened during the crash? 39. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: events leading up to the event; Patient: I was riding down [CITY]. A car suddenly veered ahead and I hit it. I had my helmet on. 40. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: events leading up to the event; EMT: You guessed your speed before the accident?

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Around 40 miles an hour.
We see some big wounds on your forearms and palms.
Do you mind if we take a closer look?
Not at all.
Are you feeling any neck or back pain?
Nope, just the outer parts hurt.
How do your arms and legs feel? Any numbness or weakness? 48. Secondary Assessment; extremities and back exam: Wounds, abrasions; Patient: No numbness or weakness, everything moves fine.
You seem alert and oriented to time, place, and situation. That's a GCS of 15-also called A&Ox4.
Pulse is now 68.
Still 16 respirations each minute.
Your blood pressure is 201 over 120 now.
SpO₂ remains steady at 97.
Applying a 4-lead EKG. We are monitoring now.
Blood glucose meter is at zero, but not needed for management now.

dialogue.role: iv start - extremity vein (arm or leg); EMT
dialogue.utterance: We're going to start an IV in your arm. Is that okay with you? 57. Take Interventions; take procedures: iv start - extremity vein (arm or leg); Patient: Yeah, sure.
Secure IV is in place on your IV pole.
We are going to give you some Fentanyl intravenously to help with the pain. Okay? 60. Take Interventions; give medications: fentanyl iv; Patient: Okay.
Per protocol, this is injury - general trauma management (protocol 4 - 1). We'll proceed with it.
Agreed. Let's complete any remaining assessments before moving.
Pulse has remained at 68.
Blood pressure is 201 over 120 still.
Saturation remains 97. 66. Reassessments; retake pain assessment: severity; EMT: How is the pain today? 67. Reassessments; retake pain assessment: severity; Patient: It's still rough, around a 10.
We'll be taking you to [COMPANY_NAME] for available trauma care.
Can you hold still for us while we pivot and secure you onto the stretcher?
Sure.
Securely in place, rails are up, and we're ready to transport.
During the ride, we'll recheck your vitals and pain level to make sure we're on track.
We are en route to the hospital with no significant changes noted.
Thank you for helping us, Mike. We're getting you to the trauma center now.
Your skin is warm which is a good sign.

You seem alert and oriented to time, place, and person. 77. Secondary Assessment; skin exam: Color; EMT: Skin shows no jaundice or pallor. 78. Secondary Assessment; head and neck exam: Pupillary reflex; EMT: Pupils are equal and reactive to light.
Your neck veins look normal with no jugular venous distension.
You have strong pulses in each limb.
I don't see any signs of deformities or angulation.
No rashes or hives noted.
Hemorrhage; Partner: I don't see any nosebleeds or blood in the ears.
Drainage; Partner: No fluid or discharge from the ears and nose.
Hearing; Partner: You can hear us clearly and respond.
Your central pulse is strong and matches peripheral pulses.
Breath sounds are clear and not diminished.
No additional heart sounds were heard with monitoring.
Bowel sounds are present and normal. 90. Secondary Assessment; abdomen exam: Masses; EMT: Your abdominal contour looks normal with no masses felt.
You don't seem to be having any difficulty urinating. 92. Secondary Assessment; genitourinary exam: Stool appearance; EMT: No signs of rectal bleeding or abnormal stools present.
We didn't find any vertebral column pain.
There is no nuchal rigidity or neck stiffness noted.

Skin turgor is normal with minimal tenting when pinched. 96. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: last oral intake; EMT: When did you last eat or drink something? 97. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: last oral intake; Patient: I had something for lunch about 4 hours ago.

What medications are you currently taking? 99. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; Patient: Not sure with this injury, but I wasn't taking anything special. 100. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: allergies; EMT: Are you allergic to anything?
I'm allergic to ibuprofen.
I don't see any traumatic scars or markings on your head or neck.
Blood sugar is not needed for monitoring at this time since it was negative and not affecting treatment.
Let's make sure everything is in place for the ride.
Your IV is secure and Fentanyl treatment is ongoing.
EKG monitor shows a sinus rhythm still.
Do you have any other medical conditions?
No, just that mild asthma which I don't need to take much of.
EMT, everything seems to be progressing as per protocol.
[COMPANY_NAME] is the best choice for trauma care given your situation.
We'll make sure you're comfortable and safe during the ride.
Once we reach [COMPANY_NAME], their trauma team will take over and help you further.
Our priority is your safety, so take your time and relax.
We aim to be as gentle and quick as possible for your wellbeing.
Let us know if anything changes or new symptoms begin during the ride.
Thanks, it all feels manageable with you two.
You're doing great, Mike. We're right here with you.
We see a green light and prepare for a smooth siren and light usage.
Maintaining our route efficiency and care quality throughout.
Hospital is slightly more than 10 minutes away by sirens.
We should arrive just before the next hour mark.
Sounds good, continuing our pace.
All set, we're en route to your trauma care destination.
Thanks, Mike, for cooperating so well.
Looks like the traffic is clearing.
Let's ensure all your lines and connections are secure for our trip.
Fentanyl remains within therapeutic range with current levels shown.
You are receiving high-quality emergency care on our team.
We'll strive for a smooth trip to the hospital with clear communication.
All systems are functioning well for a safe transport process.
We are clear on route and prioritizing trauma best-practices.
You can rest a bit, we're handling everything in the background.
No new symptoms to report at this time.
Your breathing, pulse, and pain remains at stable levels.
No changes in condition en route to the hospital.
Your assessment data and EKG are still being monitored consistently.
We expect to arrive intact and stable at [COMPANY_NAME].
